channel.title: Blog on Srivatsav Gorti | Staff Software Engineer
channel.description: Recent content in Blog on Srivatsav Gorti | Staff Software Engineer

item.title: How I Saved $1.2M/Year by Replacing Vendor Software
item.description:
Last quarter, my team at Warner Bros. Discovery completed a project that eliminated a $1.2M annual contract. We replaced an external vendor’s SOX compliance controls with an in-house solution.

This post explains how we approached it, the technical decisions, and lessons learned about build vs. buy.

The Situation

Our financial reporting platform was using a third-party SOX compliance tool. The product worked, but we had several problems:

  • Annual cost: $1.2M in licensing fees
  • Customization limits: We couldn’t adapt to changing compliance requirements
  • Vendor lock-in: Any enhancement request meant waiting for their roadmap
  • Integration friction: The tool was designed for generic cases, not our specific needs

My team (4 engineers) was tasked with evaluating alternatives.

item.title: Welcome to My Blog
item.description:
Welcome to My Blog

This is my first post. I’ll be sharing thoughts on:

  • Distributed systems design
  • Event-driven architecture
  • Scalability patterns
  • Engineering leadership
  • Real-world challenges at scale

More content coming soon!

item.title: Building Systems That Handle 1M Events Per Day: Lessons from the Trenches
item.description:
At Phenom, I built a reconciliation framework that processes 1 million events per day. This post is about what it takes to build systems at that scale, the mistakes we made, and the architecture that finally worked.

The Problem

We had 10+ downstream systems (data warehouses, analytics platforms, CRM, billing systems) consuming data from our integration platform. The problem:

  • Data drift: System A says “order completed,” System B says “order pending.” Which is right?
  • Silent failures: Errors didn’t surface until users reported discrepancies.
  • Manual reconciliation: Engineers spent hours each week manually investigating mismatches.

We needed a reconciliation framework that:

item.title: 10 Years of Distributed Systems: What I Wish I Knew as a Junior Engineer
item.description:
I’ve been building distributed systems for a decade now. Looking back, I see patterns—decisions that worked, mistakes I repeated, lessons that took years to learn.

This post is a letter to my junior self. Maybe it helps you too.

2015: The “It Works on My Machine” Fallacy

My first job at Cognizant, I built a backend service for Toyota dealers. Tested it locally. Passed all tests. Deployed to staging.

It failed.

item.title: Technical Leadership: How I Learned to Lead Engineering Decisions
item.description:
I’ve been leading teams for 4 years now—first at Phenom (7 engineers), now at Warner Bros. Discovery (4 engineers).

Early on, I made mistakes as a leader. I micromanaged. I made decisions in isolation. I avoided conflict.

This post is about what I learned about leading technical teams.

The Micromanagement Trap

At Phenom, when I was first promoted to lead, I did this:

Task assignment:
  - "Dev A, you work on this feature"
  - "Dev B, you work on that feature"
  - "Dev C, you're on bug fixes this sprint"

The problem:
